coredata.dc:title: Estimation of carbon dots amelioration of copper toxicity in maize studied by synchrotron radiation-FTIR
coredata.dc:creator: Dučić, Tanja
Milenković, Ivana
Mutavdžić, Dragosav
Nikolić, Miroslav
de Yuso, M. Valle Martínez
Vučinić, Željko
Algarra, Manuel
Radotić, Ksenija
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are biocompatible and non-toxic nanoparticles with chemical affinity to some heavy metals. Human activities increase soil pollution with copper. Cu is an essential microelement in plants, but excess can induce a harmful effects. In plant response to Cu, the cell wall plays an important role. This study aims to estimate possible amelioration effects of folic acid based CDs on Cu toxicity by studying the intracellular and cell wall compounds in maize (Zea mays L.) roots and leaves after 7 day-treatment in hydroponics. The sub-cellular compartmentalization and bio-macromolecular changes induced by 5 μM Cu applied alone or with CDs (167 and 500 mg/L) were studied using the Synchrotron-based Fourier transformmicro-spectroscopy (SR-FTIR) combined with X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Cu induced changes in content of cell wall polysaccharides, proteins, and lipids. The XPS detected CDs transport throughout the plants. The Cu/167CDs treatment reduced Cu concentration in the roots, possibly by complexation/trapping between the functional groups on CDs surface and Cu2+. Principal component analysis of FTIR spectra confirmed that Cu/500CDs treatment increased Cu adverse effects in most tissues but alleviated adverse Cu effects on cell wall polysaccharides in the root xylem, and on polysaccharides and proteins in leaf phloem and mesophyll.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Maize
Synchrotron radiation-FTIR
Copper
PCA
XPS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs based folic acid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science, and Technological development of the Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the ALBA synchrotron project 2017032156, Biostruct-X project, the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technological development of the Republic of Serbia (the contract No 451-03-68/2020-14/200053), and the project RTI201899668-BC22). MA thanks to ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação , through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005 - Centro de Química da Madeira - CQM+ (Madeira 14-20) and project RTI2018-099668-BC22 of Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades, Spain .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dependence of mean values of Cu concentration on the tissue type and combined Cu/CD treatment. The letters indicate differences between the treatments within the individual tissues.

A representative optical (VIS) image of a sector of the cross section of the root used in our studies: Left, treatment with 167 mg/L CDs; Right, control, enlarged corresponding part of the epidermal layer of cells. Circles in colors represent locations of measurements. FTIR spectral maps for this part of the maize root epidermis are also presented on these cross sections, showing the polysaccharide, lignin, and lipids fingerprint maps.

Averaged FTIR spectra in the spectral range characteristic for polysaccharides, lignin, and proteins, from several locations in the xylem in the maize root (A) and leaf (B) measured on the cross section, for different treatments and control plants.

Averaged FTIR spectra in the spectral range characteristic for lipids, from several locations in the outward epidermis layer (Epo) in maize root (A) and in the xylem (Xy) in the leaf (B) measured on the cross section, for different treatments and control; shown are the tissue types affected with the treatments.

Biplots constructed by the first two PCs for the FTIR spectra measured on the cross sections of maize root epidermis (Ep) (A), endodermis and cortex (En/Cor) (B), phloem and xylem (Ph/Xy) (C). Coloured points represent loadings corresponding to the wavenumbers in the range 800–1800 cm−1. Different colours indicate characteristic molecular species in the cell walls. Black and grey circles represent scores of the treatments. The inset graph presents loading plots of the first two PCs as function of the wavenumbers.

Biplots constructed by the first two PCs for the FTIR spectra measured on the cross sections of maize leaf, for epidermis (Ep) (A), and mesophyll, xylem, and phloem (Mes/Xy/Ph) (B). Colored points represent loadings corresponding to the wavenumbers in the range 800 - 1800 cm−1. Different colors indicate characteristic molecular species in the cell walls of each tissue. Scores of the treatments are represented by black, grey, and empty black circles for different treatments and control. Corresponding loading plots of the first two PCs are shown as insets in the graphs.

Biplots constructed by the first two PCs for the FTIR spectral region characteristic for lipids, measured on the cross sections of maize root (A) and leaf (B). Yellow points represent loadings corresponding to the wavenumbers in the range 2800-3050 cm−1. Scores of the treatments are represented by different black and grey symbols. The inset graph presents loading plots of the first two PCs as function of the wavenumbers.

Roots: C 1s core level spectra of the roots and taken after different treatments: (A) Control, (B) After addition of CDs (500 mg/L) (C) After addition of Cu2+ (5 μM), (D) After addition of CDs (500 mg/m) + Cu2+ (5 μM). N 1s core level spectra € Control and (F) After addition of CDs (500 mg/mL) + Cu2+ (5 μM). Leaves: C 1s core level spectra of the leaf and taken after different treatments: (G) Control and (H) After addition of CDs (500 mg/mL) + Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Estimation of carbon dots amelioration of copper toxicity in maize studied by synchrotron radiation-FTIR

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Univ. Málaga, 29007, Málaga, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Dept. Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Univ. Málaga, 29007, Málaga, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are biocompatible and non-toxic nanoparticles with chemical affinity to some heavy metals. Human activities increase soil pollution with copper. Cu is an essential microelement in plants, but excess can induce a harmful effects. In plant response to Cu, the cell wall plays an important role. This study aims to estimate possible amelioration effects of folic acid based CDs on Cu toxicity by studying the intracellular and cell wall compounds in maize (Zea mays L.) roots and leaves after 7 day-treatment in hydroponics. The sub-cellular compartmentalization and bio-macromolecular changes induced by 5 μM Cu applied alone or with CDs (167 and 500 mg/L) were studied using the Synchrotron-based Fourier transformmicro-spectroscopy (SR-FTIR) combined with X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Cu induced changes in content of cell wall polysaccharides, proteins, and lipids. The XPS detected CDs transport throughout the plants. The Cu/167CDs treatment reduced Cu concentration in the roots, possibly by complexation/trapping between the functional groups on CDs surface and Cu2+. Principal component analysis of FTIR spectra confirmed that Cu/500CDs treatment increased Cu adverse effects in most tissues but alleviated adverse Cu effects on cell wall polysaccharides in the root xylem, and on polysaccharides and proteins in leaf phloem and mesophyll.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an attractive group of nanoparticles used in several fields such as nanomedicine, bio-imaging, photo-catalysis, energy conversion [1]. They possess a high chemical stability, dispersibility in water, low photo-bleaching, and a strong biocompatibility combined with a low toxicity [2,3]. Several studies using CDs in biological systems have shown that CDs are non-toxic and can be used for cellular imaging [4]. So far, CDs have been mostly studied in the animal systems, being able to interact with proteins [5]. less abundant are the studies demonstrating their use in plants, such as wheat or citrus seedlings respectively [6,7]. There has been increasing interest in applications of CDs in agriculture to improve the growth and production of plants, including effects of CDs on nutrition assimilation by plants [8]. There are different analytical tools to follow them in the plants, as reviewed recently [9]. There are also studies regarding the cytotoxicity of CDs, carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide in different cells [10]. Besides, it has been shown that CDs may be used as sensors for detection and quantification of metal ions [11,12] and pesticides [13].

Previously, we characterized CDs by using various techniques including the laboratory ATR/FTIR spectroscopy of pure CDs [11]. In terms of synthesis, the hydrothermal approach is the most effective procedure to obtain CDs with a high-quality graphite core, and a surface with a varied functionalization of organic groups. This functionalization varies depending on the starting material, the use of heading materials (that fills heteroatoms such as N and S in its molecular structure), and the formation of ionized functional groups such as amines, amides, sulphonyl derivatives (negatively charged at the pH prevalent in the cellular compartments). Moreover, it was shown that this approach is suitable in terms of biocompatibility, their use not affecting the normal cell growth when used in vivo studies [14]. It was shown that CDs are suitable agents for cadmium complexing, based on their opposite charge [6].

Copper (Cu) is an essential microelement, necessary for normal growth and development of plants, involved in many cellular redox reactions; and an adequate regulation of cellular concentrations needs to be strongly adjusted (reviewed by Ducic and Polle) [15]. Cu concentrations in natural agricultural soils are far below the toxic levels. However, soil pollution with Cu widely occurs and is mainly attributed to human activities, such as mining and industrial wastes [16] and long-term application of Cu-containing fungicides, in particular in the vineyard soils [17].

On the cellular level, excess of Cu can induce a range of harmful effects including production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), exchange of essential metal ions from the active centers of enzymes or binding to functional groups (sulfhydryl, phosphate or histidyl groups) [18], growth inhibition, and interference with photosynthesis and respiration [19]. Cu acts as an essential cofactor of numerous proteins, so-called cupro-proteins, which perform many central functions in plant cells. Therefore, it is essential for a plant to keep a strictly controlled Cu homeostasis, an equilibrium that is both tissue and developmentally influenced. In plants, distinct systems involved in controlling the amount of free cytosolic ions have been evolved. The first strategy is the down-regulation of the ion-specific uptake system; the second strategy relies on the removal of free ions and ion-ligand complexes into the vacuoles or their efflux and deposition across plasma membranes into the apoplastic space [20]. The major sites of Cu-accumulation in plants are the chloroplast, the vacuole, the cytoplasm, and the cell wall [19–23]. In the heavy metal response, as ions sequestrate, the cell wall plays an important role. Cell wall (CW) is a rigid structure, composed of cellulose, lignin, hemicelluloses, and proteins, but its synthesis and composition can be severely affected in return by heavy metals [24]. CW polymers are able to bind divalent and trivalent metal cations through interaction with their functional groups, such as COOH, OH, or S H [25]. Also, chelated insoluble compounds can be precipitated in the apoplastic space [26]. Changes in several enzyme activities, proposed as biomarkers of the overall response of plants to metal exposure [27], occurred at lower copper concentrations than those inducing visible symptoms of toxicity (∼10 μM in the growth solution), and in this regard the toxic critical value was set at 26 mg Cu per kg In this study it was selected Cu concentration which does not induce visible toxic symptoms on plants but induces changes in biochemical markers (enzyme activities and isoenzyme profile) showing that plant is endangered. In roots, a new isoenzyme of peroxidase appeared when Cu concentration in the growth medium exceeded 0.5 μM, corresponding to tissue Cu content 12.6 mg kg DM [28].

This study was set to estimate possible amelioration effects of CDs on copper toxicity at the cellular level, by studying the intracellular and cell wall bio-macromolecules in maize (Zea mays L.) roots and leaves. The effect of CDs on Cu concentration in the plants was also monitored. The sub-cellular compartmentalization and content of organic compounds induced by 5 μM Cu applied alone or along with CDs (167 mg/L and 500 mg/L) was studied by using the Synchrotron Radiation based Fourier transform microspectroscopy (SR-FTIR) bypassing staining/labeling alterations of samples [29]. We also analyzed where the CDs accumulate in plants by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which showed different functional groups allocation [30] and surface information from CDs, distinguishing the environment from the processes being studied [31]. The possible oxidative stress induced in plants by CDs in the presence and absence of Cu was estimated by measuring total phenolic content (TPC) and total antioxidant activity (TAA).

In this study we test the hypothesis that CDs could enhance plant defense and thus alleviate metal toxicity. As far as we are aware, this information is still lacking, thus the main objective of this study is to enhance our understanding of the plant response to organic nanoparticles and possible applications in plant protection from heavy metal toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs based folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: CDs can be prepared from numerous simple organic precursor and renewable natural products through economic and large-scale processes. The CDs used in this experiment are based on folic acid. Briefly, folic acid (which comprise a complex structure where are involved a variety nitrogen organic functional groups), was submitted to an acid hydrothermal procedure at 100⁰C, to achieve carbon fluorescence nanoparticles [12], which produces sustainable nanomaterials for use as luminescent sensors in biological systems.

Maize seeds were germinated in distilled water (sown on, and covered with, filter paper moistened with 10 mL of distilled water) in Petri dishes. After germination, 10 seedlings per each treatment (control, 167 mg/L CDs, 500 mg/L CDs, 5 μM Cu, 5 μM Cu2+ 167 mg/L CDs and 5 μM Cu2+ + 500 mg/L CDs) were transferred to the plastic vessels (17.5 cm height) with modified 2.5 L KNOP/2 medium without Cu. One group of plants was exposed to mild Cu stress (5 μM CuSO4), and the other two groups were exposed to 167 or 500 mg/L CDs supplied in the growth medium, respectively. Two groups were exposed to combined 5μM Cu/167 mg/L CDs or 5μM Cu/500 mg/L CDs treatments. The treatment and control experiment were performed in four replicates. Seedlings were grown for the next 7 days under 8/16 h photoperiod. The nutrient solution was constantly aerated, and the pH was monitored. After 7 days of both treatments, the roots and leaves of controls, Cu, CDs, and Cu/CDs treated plants were cut into 10 μm thick sections using a cryo-microtome (Leica CM1850 (Nussloch, Germany), immediately deposited on the 0.5 mm thick ZnSe substrate (Crystran, Dorset, UK), lyophilized (Lyovac GT 2, Germany) and preserved over silica gel. For biochemical analyses of TPC and TAA, the leaves and roots of 5 plants (1 plant per replicate) were collected, frozen in liquid N2, and kept at −80 °C until measurements. For each treatment and control, the shoots, and roots of 3 plants were dried at 70 °C and collected for Cu determination.

The Synchrotron-based FTIR spectroscopy and microscopy was carried out at the MIRAS beamline at the Synchrotron light source ALBA (Barcelona, Spain). The FTIR micro-spectroscopic absorption maps were collected in transmission mode using an infrared microscope coupled to a FTIR Hyperion 3000 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in the range from 600−4000 cm−1. After fast raster scan of the cross-section of root by using FPA detector, we focused on the part of detected changes, i.e. at the compartments of tissues which gave the highest response to the treatments and examined them in detail by synchrotron irradiation and MCT detector, using the aperture of 10 × 10 μm to achieve the single cell resolution. Each spectrum being an average of 128 acquisition scans at 4 cm−1 spectral resolution. All treatment and control plants were prepared in four replicates. The IR images were obtained using a 64 × 64 pixel full-field focal plane array (FPA) detector (Bruker. Ettingen, Germany). Images were integrated for specific bands after 32 accumulated spectra per point at 8 cm−1 spectral resolution, and the spectra were background-corrected. From each IR image, the total absorbance image was generated by integrating within specific bands as indicated on each figure. We used the OPUS 7.5 (Bruker. Ettingen, Germany) software package for data analysis and UNSCRAMBLER X (CAMO Software, Oslo, Norway) to perform the principal component analysis.

The chemical characterization of the surface has been performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS spectra were acquired on a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II (Chanhassen, MN, USA) spectrometer using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV, and 1486.6 eV) for analyzing the core-level signals of the elements of interest with a hemispherical multichannel detector. The spectrometer energy scale was calibrated using Cu 2p 3/2, Ag 3d 5/2, and Au 4 f 7/2 photoelectron lines at 932.7, 368.3, and 84.0 eV, respectively. The sample spectra were recorded with a constant pass energy value at 29.35 eV, using a 200 μm diameter circular analysis area. The XPS spectra obtained were analyzed using PHI SmartSoft software and processed using MultiPak 9.3 package (Chanhassen, MN, USA). Binding energy values were referenced to adventitious carbon C 1s signal (284.8 eV). Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies.

The roots were washed with deionized water and dried. Pulverized dry plant material (root and leaf) were digested in a mixture of concentrated HNO3:H2O2 (3:2) in a microwave oven (Speedwave MWS-3+; Berghof Products + Instruments GmbH, Eningen, Germany). Concentrations of Cu and other mineral elements in plant samples and nutrient solution were determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Spectro-Genesis EOP II; Spectro Analytical Instruments GmbH, Kleve, Germany).

Principal component analysis (PCA) was performed using the Unscrambler 10.4 software (Camo A/S, Oslo, Norway) for analysis of FTIR spectra as an explorative analysis to reduce the dimensionality of the spectral data into a small number of components, for the purpose of spotting and revealing structural differences in intracellular and CW organic compounds between tissue in leaf and root samples that were treated with Cu and Cu/CDs, and control plants. PCA is a mathematical procedure for reducing dimensionality in a set of data into orthogonal components whose linear combinations approximate the original data to any desired degree of accuracy [32]. The input matrices for PCA were matrices with FTIR spectra, which rows corresponded to the samples (objects) and columns corresponded to the wavenumbers (variables). Each spectrum in the matrices corresponded to one tissue type and one treatment or control and was obtained as the mean spectrum from all points at which the spectra were recorded. The most important result of PCA is the biplot on which the objects are displayed as points and variables as vectors. The angles that these vectors form, can serve to interpret the correlation between variables, but in this study the goal was to identify the similarities/dissimilarities of individual samples, as well as to identify the range of wavenumbers that are most responsible for these similarities/dissimilarities. As the input matrix had many wavenumbers (variables), the great number of displaying vectors would make the biplot too cultured, thus the vectors from the biplot were omitted.

For investigation of impact of tissue type - root and leaf and treatment on parameter of interest - plant dry weight and Cu concentration in plants (n = 3), TAA and TPC (n = 5), two-way factor analysis of variance with balanced design was performed using the program SPSS Statistics 25 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY) which mathematically can be expressed as: can be expressed as: X_ijk=μ+α_i+β_j+(αβ)_ij+ε_ijk, where X_ijk is parameter value on i-th tissue, j-th treatment and k-th repetition; μ is total mean, α_i is efect due i-th tissue, β_j is efect due j-th treatment, (αβ)_ij is interaction effect between i-th tissue and j-th treatment, while ε_ijk is random error with normal distribution and equal variances. For post-hoc analysis, Duncan test was used. All tests were performed with a 5 % level of significance.

Cu is a crucial element in various processes in the cell wall, such as wall synthesis and loosening [33]. However, if present in excess, alleviation of Cu toxicity could be ameliorated in various ways, as for example by the addition of organic compounds, cations (Ca2+, Mg2+) or silicon [34–36].No test of the alleviation of Cu toxic effects by organic nanoparticles on plants has been reported. There are reports of positive (nano-fertilizers or nano-encapsulated nutrients) or negative (toxic effects on photosynthesis and metabolic processes) effects of nanomaterials and inorganic or organic nanotubes in agriculture [37].

In the herein study, no visual tissue damage or inhibition of plant growth was observed following the treatment of plants with CDs. However, the Cu concentration in roots was higher in both Cu and Cu/500CDs treatment compared to the control. It is obvious that application of CDs reduced Cu concentration at root level; more effective was Cu/167CDs treatment, while no significant effect was observed in leaves (Fig. 1 ).

Cell wall (CW) of epidermis and other tissues is the first and the main target of heavy metals, this compartment was examined in detail. CW is the primary site of plant contact with mineral nutrients and is characterized by a composite structure made of lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose, and proteins embedded in a matrix of pectin. We employed the SR-FTIR spectro-microscopy to observe the major CW polymers through their fingerprint infrared bands. In particular we analyzed cellulose (following bands absorption at 1160 cm−1 which indicate the C–O–C bridge stretching vibration, 1317 the CH2 wagging vibration, 1370 the C–H bending vibration, 1424 the COH bending vibration of the H2C−OH group); lignin (1510 cm−1-aromatic skeletal vibration, 1615 CC aromatic ring vibrations, and CO stretch, 1323–1335 condensed lignin), hemicelluloses- arabinoxylans as most abundant in maize (1035 cm C–OH bending, 1700–1740 the C=O stretching vibrations in the O=C–OH group of the glucuronic acid units), and CO vibrations of the ester groups. Besides, proteins were investigated (maximum absorption at ∼1550 cm-1- NH bending vibration, and ∼1650 - CO stretching vibration in Amide II and I, respectively). The aromatic ring CN stretching vibration at 1613-1631 cm−1 mostly originate from CDs.

Imaging cell wall polymers of the root and leaf tissue (e.g. epidermis, cortex, endodermis, xylem and phloem in roots, and epidermis, mesophyll, xylem and phloem in leaves) enabled us to observe which of them altered to the greatest degree in response to CDs and/or copper treatments. A typical FTIR image, by integration of specific bands, is shown in Fig. 2 . Here the visible image and maps of specific bio-macromolecular bands are overlapped for a location in root epidermis.

Fig. 3 shows, as an example, average FTIR spectra of fingerprint area of several locations in xylem in root and leaf, for different treatments. In the roots, an increase in polysaccharides was observed in Cu and CDs treatments, and a decrease to the level of the control in the combined treatments, without change in the protein and lignin spectral region. In the leaves, the effect of CDs on band intensities of polysaccharides, proteins and lignin varied more from tissue to tissue when compared to the control.

The biplots in Figs. 5 and 6 show the scores and loading plots, because of PCA applied on the base line- corrected, averaged spectra for each tissue, collected from the root and leaf sections, respectively, to discriminate tissue types according to the spectral bands’ characteristic for organic compounds. The objects in the plots were characterized as tissue types and treatments, and the variables were the FTIR wavenumbers. The corresponding scores for the root are presented as black and grey circles. The PCA score plots of tissue types discriminated phloem and xylem from the other tissue types, in both root and leaf. From the loadings on the biplots (spectra shown in the inset) it was possible to identify wavenumbers that were influential for the separation among the samples. In PC1 loadings, the dominant band is the spectral band at 890–1160 cm−1 (Figs. 5 and 6, insets), characteristic for polysaccharide bands which include cellulose, xyloglucan, and pectin, mostly allocated in CW. In PC2, the dominant bands are those of lignin (1500−1530 cm−1), proteins (1530–1580 and 1620−1700 cm−1), xylem (1255, 1460 cm−1), and esters/carboxylic acids (1700−1770 cm−1).

In Fig. 5, the PCA suggested that a two-component model explains 95 % of total variability (PC1 accounted for 83 % and PC2 for 12 %) for roots. Fig. 5A shows the biplot (scores corresponding to the treatments, and loadings to the wavenumbers) of the epidermal layer shown in Fig. 2. As demonstrated in Fig. 2, the epidermis of the maize roots used in our experiments consists of two layers of cells: an outward facing layer and an inward facing one, adjoining the cortical cell layer. All CDs and Cu treatments exhibited an impact on the polysaccharide, ester/carboxylic acid, and lignin bands in the epidermis. Changes in band intensities, proportional to relative content of the corresponding compounds, were reflected on the position of corresponding scores in the biplots. Lignin content was higher in the internal epidermal layer, as opposed to the ester/carboxylic acid content. Fig. 5B is a biplot showing the cortex and endodermis tissue in the roots. All treatments increase the polysaccharides, but their effect was more pronounced in the endodermis. In Cu and Cu/167CDs treated endodermis lignin is increased. The Cu/500CDs treatment exhibited the highest effect on polysaccharides (increased), esters/carboxylic acids and lignin (decreased) in both cortex and endodermis. The Cu/500CDs treatment increased the content of polysaccharides in epidermis and endodermis, and decreased that of proteins, but there was no alleviating effect, i.e., the scores of these treatments were not closer to the controls comparing to the scores of treatments with Cu alone. The 1085−1140 cm−1 band had the main effect on the separation of Cu/500CDs treatments from the other treatments and control, for epidermis and endodermis. This band is characteristic for cellulose, xyloglucan, and pectin, implying that this combined treatment influenced the content/structure of these compounds in the epidermis and endodermis. Fig. 5C represents the biplot of the root xylem and phloem, showing that higher concentration of CDs (500 mg/L) affected mostly the polysaccharides in xylem by increasing their content, while proteins increased in separate Cu and CDs (500 mg/L) treatments and decreased in Cu/500CDs treatment compared to the control. However, the combined treatment Cu/500CDs changed the polysaccharide content (Fig. S2) and restored them to the level close to the control plants (Fig. 5C). This indicates the alleviating effect of the CDs when applied with Cu, on the xylem cell wall structure in roots. No effect of either lower concentration of CDs or Cu on the phloem could be observed. At higher CDs concentrations (500 mg/L), either in the absence or presence of Cu the protein concentration decreased, implying mostly negative effect on cellular biochemistry.

In Fig. 6, the PCA suggested that a two-component model explains 94 % of total variability (PC1 accounted for 91 % and PC2 for 3 %) for the leaves. As shown in the biplot (Fig. 6A), in the leaf outward epidermis the treatments had different effects on polysaccharides, while in the inward epidermis layer all treatments increased their band intensity. Fig. 6B shows the biplot of phloem, xylem, and mesophyll. The most pronounced effect of the treatments was observed in xylem in comparison to the control plants’ score. In this tissue, all treatments exhibited a negative impact on the level of polysaccharides, proteins, and cellulose compared to the control, based on the notable changes in polymers’ band intensities. In mesophyll and phloem, the effect of the treatments was not as pronounced, based on the location of their scores in the same parts of the graph. The effect of the treatments with combined Cu and CDs was weaker than the effect of the treatment with Cu alone. Combined Cu/500CDs treatment raised the polysaccharides and proteins back to the levels of the control, as compared to the effect of Cu alone. In xylem, phloem, and epidermis, all treatments induced an increase in the lignin and decrease in the ester/carboxylic acid bands, compared to the control.

To analyse the effect of CDs on lipids, we monitored the region 2800−3050 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra, as shown in the example in Figs. 2 and 4. The biplots and corresponding loading plots for all considered tissue regions in roots and leaves are shown in Fig. 7 A and B, respectively. It is obvious that in roots, the Cu/500CDs treatment for both the outward and inward facing epidermal cell layers is different from the rest, showing a higher lipid content compared to the control, indicating that this treatment exhibited a strong effect on the lipids in these tissues. All the other treatments did not exhibit such a pronounced effect on lipids, compared to the control. In leaves, scores of phloem and xylem of the control are notably separated from all the treatments, indicating that all treatments had strong influence on lipids in inner tissue types. The treatments did not have such effect on the other tissues. The FTIR results demonstrate a distinct effect of Cu alone or in combination with CDs on the content of CW polymers and other bio-macromolecules, which differed depending on tissue type and molecular species (Figs. 2 and S2). The effects of Cu/CDs treatments, especially when high concentrations of CDs were applied, on all CW polymers’ bands, were significant. The results show that the epidermal layers in both root and leaf are more affected by all treatments. As for the combined treatments, in the root and leaf epidermal layers, as well as in root cortex and endodermis, both Cu/167CDs and Cu/500CDs treatments changed considerably the content of all CW polymers as well as lipids, so their effect was like the effect of Cu alone. However, the 500 mg/L CDs, when applied in combination with Cu, in root xylem reduced the Cu induced changes in CW polysaccharides content, and in leaf phloem and mesophyll changed polysaccharides and proteins content, to the level of the control. This could be interpreted as an alleviating effect of CDs on the metal-induced changes of the CW composition. These results indicate that the alleviating effect of combined Cu/CDs treatments is exerted on different bio-polymer types in roots and leaves, but in both organs the effects are in the internal tissues. The lipids are affected in external root tissue (epidermis) by the combined Cu/500CDs treatment and in internal leaf tissues (xylem and phloem) by all treatments, which might be due to oxidative damages caused by the treatments or by binding the metal without or with CDs. These results confirm that membranes are more vulnerable to the treatments, while the CW acts as a protecting compartment, in particular tissue types. Since the effects were measured in the plants after one-week treatment duration, they may be considered as plant response through the changed synthesis of the CW polymers.

It was shown that 5 μM copper induced the increase in peroxidase activity in maize [28], related to oxidative reactions corresponding to an increase in peroxides and free radicals in the plant cells [38]. Previous studies have highlighted that Cu-treatments concomitantly affect both lignin metabolism and peroxidase activities [39,40]. Thus, the presence of 100 μM Cu in the growth medium in maize altered the peroxidase isoenzyme forms in the roots and inhibited the oxidative cycle of the peroxidases [41].

This may have consequences on ROS production, oxidative stress, and CW organization, especially on lignin synthesis and its connections with polysaccharides and proteins. The presence of Cu on the surface of the protein may facilitate their participation in ROS production [42]. One class of Cu proteins located in CW are the plantacyanin, which are classified as type I copper proteins facilitating the transfer of electrons between a donor and an acceptor [43,44]. There is a crosstalk between Cu availability and plantacyanin transcript accumulation [44,45].

The Cu/500CDs combined treatment alleviates adverse Cu effects on CW proteins (raised the proteins back to the levels close to the control, as compared to the effect of Cu alone) in leaf phloem and mesophyll. Nevertheless, the biochemical tests in the present study indicate that the CDs alone did not induce any severe oxidative stress in plants, except that 500CDs induced a change of TAA in leaves. In the present study, the excessive production of ROS induced by 5 μM Cu activated plant defence system leading to the increase of TPC and TAA in plant roots and leaves. The CDs in combined Cu/CDs treatments were not able to cancel this effect caused by Cu. Since TPC is an indicator of a part of the plant defence, the obtained results (Fig. S1) indicate that CDs do not induce a response through higher production of phenolics in treated plants. The fact that 500 mg/L CDs induced an increase in TAA in the leaves may indicate that CDs in higher concentration induce an increase in some ROS, possible through the interaction with proteins or lipids in membranes, which was documented by the changes in their FT-IR band intensities (Figs. 3–5).

XPS analysis provided information on the presence of Cu2+ and CDs nanoparticles (500 mg/mL) and their effect on the surface chemical composition of the sample sections from maize roots and leaves. According to the analysis of the C 1s core level spectra (Fig. 8 ), all samples showed three main contributions associated to the lipidic part of the membrane cell structure (from C–C/C–H groups, fatty acids of the cutin component) at 284.8 eV, and the cellulosic component, associated to the C–OH /C–O–C/C–N functional groups at 286.5 eV [46]. The acyl group, which represents the protein component, is associated to the presence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups (C=O and O–C=O, respectively; represented by two peaks at 287.6 and 288.5 eV) [47]. To understand the changes, the area ratio (in parenthesis) between the adventitious carbon signal, taken by reference at 284.8 eV, and the other components is depicted in Table S2 for the four analysed samples. An increase in this ratio was observed when CDs (500 mg/mL) are present in the media, from 2.34 (Ratio C–C:C–H / C–OH:C–O–C:C–N) (Fig. 8A, Table S2) in control to 12.31 (Fig. 8B) This effect of CDs was maintained when the plants are treated with Cu2+ (5 μM), the ratio slightly increased to 2.93, which may be explained with the interaction between C–O/C–N and Cu2+ (Fig. 8C, Table S2), and retained nearly constant (2.70) when plants are treated by Cu2+ (5 μM) / CDs (500 mg/mL) (Fig. 8D). The ratio concerning carbonyl and carboxyl groups in the presence of Cu2+/CDs increases from 7.22 in control to 11.86 for CO and from 9.44 in control to 14.25 for O–C=O, clearly demonstrating the interaction between the CD-Cu2+ complex and these groups. This is in an agreement with the PCA result of FTIR spectra for all root tissue parts (Fig. 5), showing the ester (carbonyl) and carboxyl acid bands decrease after CDs and Cu treatments.

The analysis of high-resolution spectra for N 1s signals showed two main contributions at 399.1 to the amine and amide (-NH2/−CONH2) respectively and a band at 401.1 eV [48] assigned to the protonated amine (H+-NH2) (Fig. 8E, F) [48]]. The amine/amide ratio decreased from 1.7 for the control to 1.50 for the treated plants with Cu2+ (5 μM) / CDs (500 mg/mL) (Table S2), which indicated the change of the environment of the C–O/C–N bonds, in other terms the justification that CDs complexes the Cu2+ ions by these surface functional groups in the root’s cells.

Based on this argument, inset in Fig. 8F proposes a scheme of the complexation/trapping effect between the functional organic groups on the surface of the CDs and Cu2+. These results demonstrate that Cu-CDs complex is stable inside the plants. As for the signals O 1s, P 2p and S2p, no appreciable differences could be observed. The analysis of the leaf sections did not detect Cu signals, which was confirmed by the ICP analysis, showing considerably lower Cu concentration in the leaves comparing with the roots (Fig. 1). This is in accordance with the literary data [49,50]. The atomic composition showed signals with significant changes due to the C 1s. Both spectra can be deconvoluted into three main signals at 284.8 (C–C/C–H), 286.3 (C–O/C–N) and 287.6 eV (C=O) respectively, as observed in the control sample (Fig. 8G). The ratio for the treatment with CDs (500 mg/mL) (Fig. 8H) increased from 25.07 for control to 34.66 % for the first signal; decreased from 72.8–62.2 % for the second signal and a slightly increased from 2.1–3.2 % for the third signal, all these changes pointed out the presence of CDs in leaves. No spectral changes for O 1 s and N 1 s were observed.

In conclusion, the XPS results in this study show that the CDs were taken up by the plants and translocated to the leaves. The CDs concentrations employed did not exhibit any negative effects on plant growth; they even stimulated the increase in biomass (Fig. S3).

The ICP results indicate that 167 mg/L CDs considerably reduced Cu concentration in the roots compared to the treatment with Cu alone (Fig. 1), which may be explained through CDs mediated interaction of Cu with CO and O–C=O groups, supported by XPS analysis (Table S2, Fig. 8) and FTIR spectra (Fig. 5). This is in accordance with the increase in root dry weight following the treatment with the lower CDs concentration (Fig. S1). It is a very important effect since it is known adverse effects of Cu on the root [51,52]. The higher CDs concentration was not effective in the reduction of Cu concentration, arguing the optimal doses of CDs. The decrease in N, O, and P, as well as the increase in S induced by CDs, may be explained by the hormetic effect, i.e. the physiological zone, there is generally favorable biological response to low exposures to toxins and other stressors. Glutathione, which contains sulfur as an antioxidant in plants, has a dual role in response to metal stress, as an antioxidant and precursor for chelators of metals.

It has been suggested that the interplay between phytochelatins production and glutathione may itself cause oxidative stress [53,54]. Nevertheless, both CDs concentrations, as well as Cu alone induced changes in all macromolecules in CWs, to a different extent. The XPS results follow the FTIR data since binding of CDs to the groups shown in Table S2 could be related to the changes in FTIR bands of proteins, lignin, esters/carboxylic acids, polysaccharides, and lipids. The CDs could adsorb onto the surface of the CW polymers and in addition they could bind Cu2+ on the surface (see the scheme in Fig. 8F). The interaction of Cu/500CDs with CO and O–C=O (Table S2) indicates that CDs facilitate Cu2+ interaction with lipids and polysaccharides, which was shown to have an impact on CW polymers or membrane lipids. This was especially pronounced in the CW structure of the root xylem and leaf phloem cells since they contain the highest amount of the secondary CWs and thus of polysaccharides that are able to interact with CDs. This is important because of the role of vascular elements in the transport of organic matter from roots to leaves. Since mesophyll is a part of the leaf crucial for photosynthetic activity, the positive effect of the combined CDs/Cu treatments on CW polymers may have a protective impact.

Future work is to show if the organic CDs based on folic acid may be successful in amelioration of the adverse effects of the other metals accumulated in plants from the environments due to human activities or to the natural presence in certain soils.

CDs based on folic acid were transported through maize and were present in both roots and leaves after 7 day-treatment in hydroponics. There were no visual adverse effects on plants. The root dry weight increased after the Cu/167CDs treatment, and leaf dry weight after both Cu/167CDs and Cu/500CDs treatments compared to the control. By using the Synchrotron-based FTIR micro-spectroscopy in combination with statistical analysis and the XPS, we observed that treatment with Cu/167CDs significantly reduced Cu concentration in the roots, in parallel with the increase in root dry weight. Higher CDs concentration applied together with Cu increased the adverse effects of Cu in most tissues, also documented by the decrease in root dry weight and increase in TPC and TAA. However, the Cu/500CDs combined treatment alleviated adverse effects of excess Cu on CW polysaccharides in the root xylem, and on polysaccharides and proteins in leaf phloem and mesophyll. The lipids were affected by Cu/500CDs treatment in the root epidermis and by all treatments in leaf xylem, phloem, and mesophyll, showing the higher vulnerability of the membranes in the root outward tissues and in the leaf inward tissues. However, application of CDs was not effective in mitigation of the oxidative stress in both roots and leaves induced by excess Cu.

T. Dučić: Investigation, Formal Analysis, Methodology, Writing-original draft. I. Milenković: Investigation, Formal Analysis, Methodology. D. Mutavdžić: Formal Analysis, Methodology. M. Nikolić: Formal Analysis. M.V. de Yuso: Formal Analysis. Ž. Vučinić: Investigation, writing. M. Algarra: Investigation, Formal Analysis, Methodology, Writing-original draft. K. Radotić: Investigation, Formal Analysis, Methodology, Writing-original draft.

This work was supported by the ALBA synchrotron project 2017032156, Biostruct-X project, the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technological development of the Republic of Serbia (the contract No 451-03-68/2020-14/200053), and the project RTI201899668-BC22). MA thanks to ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação, through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005 - Centro de Química da Madeira - CQM+ (Madeira 14-20) and project RTI2018-099668-BC22 of Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades, Spain.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the ALBA synchrotron light source for beam time allocation and good working conditions. We are thankful to Dr. K. Smiljanić for cryo-sectioning of the plant tissues.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.111828.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
